section.title: How Majestic Is Your Name
section.sectionRationale: A hymn of pure praise framed by a repeated refrain, moving from the majesty of God's name displayed in creation and even in infants' mouths, to wonder that this same God crowns frail humanity with glory and dominion over His creation.
section.thesis: The LORD's name is majestic in all the earth, His glory set above the heavens, yet this same God is mindful of small, mortal humanity, crowning them with glory and honor and placing all creation under their feet.
section.frameNote: A hymn framed by an opening and closing refrain (vv1, 9), moving through God's glory silencing the enemy through the mouths of infants (v2), wonder at the heavens and human smallness (vv3-4), and the crowning of humanity with dominion (vv5-8).

verses.text: O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is Your name in all the earth! You have set Your glory above the heavens.
From the mouths of children and infants You have ordained praise on account of Your adversaries, to silence the enemy and avenger.

units.label: What is man?
units.note: the son of man that You care for him

verses.text: When I behold Your heavens, the work of Your fingers, the moon and the stars, which You have set in place,
what is {{adam|man}} that You are mindful of him, or the {{benAdam|son of man}} that You care for him?
You made him a little lower than the angels; You crowned him with {{kavod|glory}} and honor.
You made him ruler of the works of Your hands; You have placed everything under his feet:
all sheep and oxen, and even the beasts of the field,
the birds of the air and the fish of the sea, all that swim the paths of the seas.
O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is Your name in all the earth!

adam.gloss: Man, humankind, the same word used for the first man in Genesis. Here it names the sheer wonder that the God whose glory towers over the heavens is mindful of humanity at all, small and mortal as we are.

benAdam.gloss: Son of man, a Hebrew phrase for a human being, emphasizing frailty and mortality. Jesus takes this phrase as His most common self-designation in the Gospels, and Hebrews 2:6-9 quotes this very verse of Christ, who was made lower than the angels and is now crowned with glory and honor.

kavod.gloss: Glory, honor, weight or substance, used both of God's own majesty, verse 1, and of the crown given to humanity, verse 5. Humanity's glory is derivative, a reflected honor granted by the God whose own glory is set above the heavens.

basic.context: Psalm 8 is a pure hymn of praise, framed by the same declaration at its start and end: how majestic is Your name in all the earth. Between the two refrains, David marvels that the God whose glory fills the heavens is mindful of small, mortal humanity, crowning them with glory and giving them dominion over the rest of creation.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm meditates on Genesis 1's creation account, humanity made in God's image and given dominion, turning it into worship; the phrase 'son of man' here describes ordinary human frailty, but Hebrews 2 shows the New Testament reading this psalm as ultimately fulfilled in Jesus, the true and greater Son of Man.
basic.originalLanguages: David marvels that adam, humanity, and ben-adam, son of man, emphasizing frailty and smallness, are the object of God's mindful care. Humanity is crowned with kavod, glory or honor, the same weighty word used of God's own majesty in verse 1, a glory that is given, not inherent.
basic.typology: Humanity's original calling to have dominion over creation, though marred by the fall, anticipates Jesus, the true Son of Man, who alone fulfills this psalm completely, all things now placed under His feet, Hebrews 2:8-9.
basic.christ: Hebrews 2:6-9 quotes this psalm directly of Jesus: made lower than the angels for a little while, through His incarnation and suffering, and now crowned with glory and honor because He tasted death for everyone. What Adam forfeited, Christ, the last Adam, has secured for humanity forever.
basic.god: God is majestic beyond the heavens, yet His glory is displayed even in the simple praise of children, silencing His adversaries not with force but with the weakest human voices, showing that His power delights to work through apparent weakness.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 1:26-28 gives humanity dominion over creation, the background this psalm celebrates. Matthew 21:16 quotes verse 2 of children praising Jesus in the temple. Hebrews 2:6-9 applies this whole psalm directly to Christ's incarnation, death, and exaltation.
basic.oneStory: Psalm 8 marvels that the majestic Creator crowns frail humanity with glory and dominion, a calling humanity forfeited in the fall but that Jesus, the true Son of Man, has fully recovered and secured, so that everything is indeed now, or will be, placed under His feet.

context.literary: A hymn of praise structured with a clear inclusio, the same refrain opening and closing the psalm, moving from cosmic scale, God's glory above the heavens, down to the smallest human voices, then back up through humanity's God-given dominion over creation.
context.historical: The superscription, for the choirmaster, upon the Gittith, likely names an instrument or tune, possibly from Gath, associated with several psalms of praise, reflecting its use in temple worship as a celebratory hymn.
context.canonical: Meditates directly on Genesis 1:26-28's creation mandate, becoming one of the most quoted psalms in the New Testament for its picture of humanity's, and ultimately Christ's, dominion, cited in Matthew 21:16, 1 Corinthians 15:27, Ephesians 1:22, and Hebrews 2:6-9.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To move worshippers from wonder at God's cosmic majesty to wonder at His personal care for humanity, celebrating the dignity and dominion God assigned to mortal, frail creatures as an act of pure grace rather than human merit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes humanity's created dignity and dominion; it does not prescribe specific behavior so much as it forms worship and humility, teaching believers to hold together smallness before God's vastness and dignity as His image bearers.

debates.question: Does 'son of man' in verse 4 refer only to humanity in general, or does the psalm anticipate a specific greater fulfillment?

views.label: A generic description of humanity, later applied typologically to Christ
views.case: In its original context the phrase parallels 'man' in synonymous parallelism, describing all humanity's frailty and dignity, with Hebrews 2 later applying it typologically to Christ as humanity's representative and fulfillment.

views.label: A description whose full weight was always destined for the ideal, greater Son of Man
views.case: Since no ordinary human has ever actually had 'all things' placed under their feet, some read the psalm as reaching, even in its original sense, toward an ultimate human figure who would fully realize this dominion, which Hebrews 2:8 explicitly notes 'we do not yet see' fulfilled in humanity generally.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the New Testament rightly applies this psalm fully and finally to Christ, the Son of Man who alone has all things placed under His feet, without denying the psalm's original celebration of humanity's God-given dignity.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm marvels that adam and ben-adam, humanity and son of man, humble, mortal terms, are nonetheless mindful objects of God's paqad, His attentive care. Humanity is crowned with kavod, glory, and hadar, honor or splendor, the same vocabulary elsewhere reserved for kings and for God Himself, showing the astonishing dignity bestowed on frail creatures.

typology.type: Humanity crowned with glory and given dominion over creation, Psalm 8:5-6
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Son of Man, made lower than the angels for a little while and now crowned with glory and honor, with all things placed under His feet, Hebrews 2:6-9, 1 Corinthians 15:27.

study.christ: Hebrews 2:6-9 quotes Psalm 8 directly of Jesus: for a little while made lower than the angels, through His incarnation and suffering unto death, and now crowned with glory and honor because He tasted death for everyone. What humanity forfeited through the fall, dominion marred by sin and death, Christ the last Adam has recovered and secured forever, so that all things, though not yet visibly, are being placed under His feet, and through Him, under the feet of all who are united to Him.
study.god: God's majesty fills the heavens, yet He is mindful of the smallest and weakest, silencing His adversaries through the praise of infants rather than displays of raw power, showing that His strength is often most visible in what the world considers insignificant.

crossReferences.note: The creation mandate giving humanity dominion over creation, the foundation this psalm celebrates in song.
Jesus quotes Psalm 8:2 when children praise Him in the temple, applying the psalm's language directly to Himself.
Applies this entire psalm to Christ's incarnation, suffering, death, and exaltation with all things under His feet.

study.oneStory: Psalm 8 marvels that the majestic Creator crowns frail, mortal humanity with glory and dominion over His creation. That calling, forfeited in Eden, is fully recovered in Jesus Christ, the true Son of Man, crowned with glory and honor, with all things now being placed under His feet for the sake of all who belong to Him.

sermon.title: How Majestic Is Your Name
sermon.why: Begg examines Psalm 8's right reflection on both God and man, urging big thoughts of the Creator and small thoughts of ourselves, and shows how seeing God and our sins rightly leads us to be reconciled to Him through Christ.

quiz.q: What is the refrain that opens and closes Psalm 8?

options.text: O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is Your name in all the earth.
options.note: This exact declaration frames the whole psalm as an inclusio.

options.text: The heavens declare the glory of God.
options.note: This is the opening line of Psalm 19, a related but different psalm.

options.text: Blessed is the man who fears the LORD.
options.note: This phrase belongs to other psalms, such as Psalm 112, not Psalm 8.

options.text: The psalm has no refrain.
options.note: Verses 1 and 9 are identical, forming a clear refrain.

quiz.q: How does God silence the enemy and avenger, according to verse 2?

options.text: Through the praise from the mouths of children and infants.
options.note: This surprising image shows God's power displayed through the weakest human voices.

options.text: Through the might of Israel's armies.
options.note: The verse specifically credits the praise of infants and children, not military force.

options.text: Through natural disasters.
options.note: No natural disaster is mentioned; the specific means named is the praise of children.

options.text: The psalm does not explain how the enemy is silenced.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly names the praise of children and infants as the means.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist marvel at in verses 3-4?

options.text: That God, whose heavens are the work of His fingers, is mindful of and cares for small, mortal humanity.
options.note: This is the psalm's central wonder, the contrast between cosmic vastness and personal care.

options.text: That the stars are more numerous than he expected.
options.note: The wonder is specifically about God's care for humanity in light of the heavens' vastness, not merely counting stars.

options.text: That humanity has built impressive cities.
options.note: Human achievement is not the psalm's focus; God's care despite human smallness is.

options.text: Nothing in particular; these verses are purely descriptive.
options.note: Verses 3-4 pose a direct, wondering question about God's mindfulness of humanity.

quiz.q: What has God given to humanity, according to verses 5-8?

options.text: Glory and honor, and dominion over the works of His hands, including all animals, birds, and fish.
options.note: This is the psalm's description of humanity's God-given dignity and rule, echoing Genesis 1.

options.text: Immortality and freedom from all suffering.
options.note: The psalm describes dominion and glory, not a promise of immortality or freedom from suffering.

options.text: Wealth and political power over other nations.
options.note: The dominion described is over creation, animals and creatures, not political rule over nations.

options.text: Nothing; humanity is left with no special role.
options.note: Verses 5-8 explicitly describe a significant, God-given role of dominion and honor.

quiz.q: How does Hebrews 2:6-9 use this psalm?

options.text: It applies the psalm directly to Jesus, made lower than the angels for a little while and now crowned with glory and honor because He tasted death for everyone.
options.note: This is a direct New Testament application of Psalm 8 to Christ's incarnation and exaltation.

options.text: It rejects Psalm 8 as no longer applicable after Christ's coming.
options.note: Hebrews 2 embraces and fulfills Psalm 8's language in Christ, rather than rejecting it.

options.text: It applies Psalm 8 only to angels, not to humanity or Christ.
options.note: Hebrews 2 specifically contrasts Christ's temporary lowering beneath the angels with His subsequent exaltation, applying the psalm to Him, not to angels.

options.text: It has no connection to Psalm 8 at all.
options.note: Hebrews 2:6-8 directly quotes Psalm 8:4-6 and applies it to Christ.

questions: The psalm moves from the vastness of God's glory to wonder at His care for small, mortal humanity. Where in your life do you need to be reminded that God's greatness does not make Him distant or indifferent to you personally?

God silences His adversaries through the praise of children and infants, not through displays of raw power. Where might God be using something small or seemingly weak in your life to accomplish something significant?

Humanity's glory and dominion are described as given by God, not earned or inherent. How does remembering that your dignity comes from God's gift, not your own achievement, change the way you view your worth or your work?

Jesus is the true Son of Man who fulfills this psalm completely, with all things ultimately placed under His feet. How does trusting that Christ's reign is secure change the way you face situations that currently feel out of control?

The psalm begins and ends with the same declaration of God's majestic name. What would it look like for your own days this week to begin and end with genuine wonder at who God is, rather than being framed only by your own tasks and worries?

translationNotes.note: Upon the Gittith likely names a musical instrument or tune associated with Gath, appearing in the superscriptions of three psalms, 8, 81, and 84, though its exact meaning is uncertain.

A little lower than the angels renders Elohim, which can mean God, gods, or heavenly beings; the Septuagint and Hebrews 2:7 both render it angels, the traditional and most widely followed translation.

Son of man, ben-adam, is a common Hebrew idiom for a human being, emphasizing mortality and frailty; Jesus later adopts 'Son of Man' as His most frequent self-designation in the Gospels, drawing on both this psalm and Daniel 7:13-14.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 8:1-9. No em or en dashes appear in this psalm's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file. Sermon link verified this session by fetching the Truth For Life sermon page for Psalm 8.
